score_averaging_note: Records with score_status='invalid' are infrastructure/judge/synthesis failures and must not be averaged as model-quality scores.

answer_excerpt:
The admin login screen is implemented in `src/pages/Login.js` as the primary authentication entry point for the dashboard application. The component authenticates admin users against a Firebase Realtime Database by querying the `admin` node, filtering out any entry with the key `"test"`, and matching the provided email and password against stored credentials. Email validation uses a standard regex pattern (`/^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/`), while password validation requires a minimum length of 6 characters. Upon successful authentication, the component checks that `foundUser.status === "active"` before allowing access; inactive accounts display a message directing users to contact the Master Admin. On successful login, three values are stored in localStorage: `currentUser` (the full user object), `isLoggedIn` (set to `"true"`), and `userRole` (the user's role from the database). The user is then navigated to `/dashboard` with the user object passed via router state.

The routing configuration in `src/App.js` places the login page at the root path (`/`), making it the default landing route for the application. A separate `ForgotPassword` page exists at `/forgot-password` but contains only static UI content instructing users to contact the master adminâ€”there is no actual password reset logic implemented. Error handling covers scenarios including email not found, incorrect password, inactive accounts, missing admin users in the database, and database connection failures. The login form uses Bootstrap for styling with visual feedback through `is-valid` and `is-invalid` CSS classes applied on blur and change events. There is also a mock users file (`src/data/mockUsers.js`) containing three test accounts with plain-text passwords intended solely for development purposes, though this file is not referenced in the actual login logic.

The left sidebar navigation component in this codebase is `src/components/Sidebar.js`. It is a functional React component exported as default that renders the admin dashboard sidebar. The component uses the `UserContext` to access authentication state and guards against unauthenticated access by returning `null` when no `currentUser` is present. Navigation links are organized into three sections separated by `<hr>` elements: core features, analytics, and settings. The sidebar uses icon-driven navigation items imported from `react-icons/fa` including icons for home, user check, users, toolbox, chart, settings, and help. Active route styling is handled by `NavLink` from `react-router-dom`, which automatically applies active classes based on the current route. The component depends on Bootstrap utilities for styling and renders a logo image from `../assets/handyman_fakeLogo.png` along with an `AdminProfileHeader` component in the footer area. Styling is applied via `../styles/Sidebar.css`. The sidebar renders `null` for unauthenticated users rather than showing a login redirect, which could be improved by implementing a proper redirect to the login page as mentioned in the component comments.

answer_excerpt:
The primary file handling user engagement metrics is `UserEngagement.js`, a React page component that fetches 2025 user engagement data from Firebase Realtime Database and renders it as metric summary cards along with a monthly engagement overview table. The component retrieves data from the `userEngagement/2025` path in Firebase and processes it to calculate derived metrics including returning users, average session time, and bounce rate.

The raw metrics collected from Firebase include DAU (Daily Active Users), MAU (Monthly Active Users), new users, total session time, session count, and bounce count. From these raw values, the component derives returningUsers as `MAU - newUsers`, average session time as `totalSessionTime / sessionCount`, and bounce rate as `(bounceCount / sessionCount) * 100`. The auto-selected "current month" is determined by finding the month with the highest DAU value across all available data.

The UI displays six key engagement metrics as stat cards: DAU, MAU, New Users, Returning Users, Average Session Time (in minutes), and Bounce Rate percentage. Each metric card is paired with an icon from react-icons and styled using Bootstrap classes for layout within a responsive grid. The monthly table presents all available months' data with tooltip support for each column header, and the current month row is highlighted using Bootstrap's `table-active` class.

Bootstrap tooltips are re-initialized via a `useEffect` hook whenever `monthlyData` changes, using the standard Bootstrap tooltip API on elements with `data-bs-toggle="tooltip"` attributes. The component also reads the current user from localStorage to pass to the StickyHeader component for authentication context. ServiceAnalytics.js includes related metrics like `totalLogins` and `newUsers` from the `serviceAnalytics/2025` path, but UserEngagement.js is the dedicated page for the specific engagement metrics you asked about.

The job management functionality in this codebase is primarily implemented in `src/pages/JobManagement.js`, which is a React page component that handles CRUD operations for job listings, integrating with Firebase Realtime Database. The component fetches job data from the `DummyJob` path in Firebase but writes updates to the `Job/` path for cross-app compatibility. It uses `react-bootstrap` components (Table, Form, Modal, etc.) along with custom components like `PaginationControls`, `StickyHeader`, `ConfirmModal`, and `ExportReportButton`.

The page provides multi-criteria filtering capabilities, allowing users to filter by job status, job category, and a general search term that matches against category, location, description, and job ID. The `filteredJobs` state is computed by checking if each job matches all three filter criteria simultaneously. Pagination is implemented with configurable entries per page (defaulting to 10), with the component calculating `startIndex` and slicing the filtered array to display only the current page's jobs.

Job fields are organized into logical groups (General, Location, DateTime, Payment, Meta) and rendered dynamically based on the `groupedFields` configuration. The `getInputType` function determines input types dynamicallyâ€”returning "date" for fields containing "date", "time" for fields containing "time", "number" for fields containing "salary", and defaulting to "text" for all others. Some fields like `createdBy`, `createdAt`, `lastUpdated`, `jobId`, `customerId`, and `quotedHandymen` are protected from editing in edit mode.

Form validation checks that `jobCat`, `jobDesc`, `jobStatus`, `jobDateFrom`, and `jobDateTo` are all required fields before saving. The delete operation removes jobs from both `DummyJob` and `Job` paths using `Promise.all` to handle cases where different parts of the application may be using either path. There is a known issue noted in the code: a TODO comment indicates pending alignment of data field formatting with @Jenny, and there is a duplicate...

reads the `admin` node from Realtime Database

answer_excerpt:
The login authentication validation flow in this application is implemented across several files, with `src/pages/Login.js` serving as the primary component handling the entire authentication process. When a user submits the login form, the `handleLogin` function first validates the email using a regex pattern (`/^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/`) and checks that the password meets a minimum length of 6 characters. The validation occurs both on input change and on blur events, providing real-time feedback to users through Bootstrap's `is-valid` and `is-invalid` CSS classes. If client-side validation passes, the component queries Firebase Realtime Database under the `admin` node, filters out any entry with the key `"test"`, and searches for a matching email. The authentication then performs three sequential checks: whether the email exists in the database, whether the password matches the stored value, and whether the user's `status` field equals `"active"`. Inactive accounts receive a specific error message instructing users to contact the Master Admin for support. Upon successful authentication, the component stores `currentUser`, `isLoggedIn`, and `userRole` in localStorage and navigates to the dashboard. The `UserProvider` context (initialized in `src/index.js` wrapping the entire app) loads the stored `currentUser` from localStorage on mount via `useEffect`, making the user state available globally through the `useUser` hook. There is a minor XSS concern noted in the codebase: error messages containing `
` tags are rendered using `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`, which could become a vector if error content becomes user-controlled. The mock users in `mockUsers.js` are not used in the production authentication flowâ€”they exist solely for development and testing purposes with intentionally plain-text passwords.

answer_excerpt:
The admin settings page is implemented as a React component located at `src/pages/AdminSettings.js`. It provides CRUD operations for managing admin users, including create, read, update, and delete functionality along with search, filtering, and confirmation dialogs. The component depends on several libraries: react-bootstrap for UI components (Table, Button, Modal, Form, Badge, Row, Col, OverlayTrigger, Tooltip, Alert, Fade), firebase/database for real-time data operations, uuid for generating unique identifiers, and date-fns for date formatting. It uses sibling components StickyHeader and ConfirmModal for layout and destructive action confirmations respectively.

The component establishes a real-time listener on the `admin` Firebase path to keep admin data synchronized. Access control is enforced at the UI level where role-based access is restricted to "Master Admin" users. Filtering capabilities include search term matching, role filtering, and status filtering (active/inactive). Password validation enforces a minimum length of 6 characters, and the component filters out the `test` admin key from query results.

The routing configuration in App.js defines the admin settings route at `/admin-settings` within the protected wildcard route (`/*`) that wraps protected pages with the Sidebar layout. The Sidebar component provides navigation links to admin settings through its settings section, with conditional rendering that only displays navigation when a user is authenticated. Admin users authenticate against the Firebase Realtime Database, where their credentials and status (active/inactive) are validated before granting access to the application.

answer_excerpt:
The job management page component is located at `src/pages/JobManagement.js` and serves as the primary interface for managing job listings within the application. It provides full CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations for jobs, integrating directly with Firebase Realtime Database. The component fetches job data from the `DummyJob` path while writing updates to the `Job/` path, a design choice made to ensure compatibility across different parts of the application.

The component supports comprehensive filtering capabilities, allowing users to filter jobs by status, category, and free-text search across fields like category, location, description, and job ID. Filtering logic checks if each criterion matches using case-insensitive comparisons, with all three conditions required for a job to appear in results. Pagination is implemented with configurable entries per page, calculating visible items based on the current page number. Jobs are organized into logical groups (General, Location, DateTime, Payment, Meta) for display in a modal view, with dynamic input type detection that renders date pickers for date fields, time pickers for time fields, and number inputs for salary fields.

Form validation ensures required fields (job category, description, status, start date, and end date) are populated before saving. The deletion flow removes job entries from both Firebase paths simultaneously using `Promise.all` to handle the cross-path cleanup. The component uses react-bootstrap for UI elements including Table, Form, Button, Modal, and Alert components, along with custom sibling components like StickyHeader, ConfirmModal, and ExportReportButton. The code includes a noted TODO regarding pending alignment of data field formatting with @Jenny, and there is a known issue where a duplicate "Done" option exists in the jobStatus dropdown at approximately line 160.

answer_excerpt:
The Support Feedback page is implemented in `src/pages/SupportFeedback.js` and provides a modal-driven workflow for agents to reply to support tickets. The component uses real-time Firebase listeners on the `support_requests` node to keep ticket data synchronized across all connected clients. When an agent responds to a ticket, the system uses EmailJS (`@emailjs/browser`) to send a templated reply email to the ticket submitter.

The ticket reply flow is embedded within a modal interface where agents can view ticket details, compose a response, and submit it. Status transitions are tracked as tickets move from Open â†’ In Progress â†’ Resolved/Closed, with the new ticket creation using auto-generated IDs in the format `T${Date.now()}`. Search and filtering work by ticket ID or user name, and pagination displays 10 entries per page.

The component integrates with several sibling components including `StickyHeader` for page title rendering, `PaginationControls` for navigating large ticket lists, and `ExportReportButton` for generating reports. The code contains a `TOFIX` comment noting outstanding issues with modal state changes and notification handling that may need attention.

answer_excerpt:
The Service Analytics page component is located at `src/pages/ServiceAnalytics.js` and serves as the main dashboard for visualizing 2025 service metrics in the admin application. It is exported as a default component and fetches data from four Firebase Realtime Database paths: `serviceAnalytics/2025`, `Handyman`, `User`, and `Job`. The component uses four `useState` hooks to manage `chartData`, `userStats`, `jobStats`, and `ratingDistribution`.

On mount, a `useEffect` hook triggers an asynchronous `fetchData` function that retrieves all the necessary data. User statistics are calculated by counting the keys in the Handyman and User nodes, while job statistics aggregate the `jobStatus` field into three buckets: Done, In Progress, and Cancelled. The `chartData` is formatted from raw Firebase analytics using a `monthsOrder` mapping to structure entries with fields like `newHandymen`, `newCustomers`, `newUsers`, `jobsCompleted`, `averageRating`, and `totalLogins`. The rating distribution data is currently mocked because individual ratings are not yet stored in the database.

The component renders four distinct visualizations using Recharts within a Bootstrap grid layout. A PieChart displays the user ratio between Handymen and Customers, a BarChart shows the job status distribution, a second PieChart presents the rating distribution, and a stacked BarChart visualizes user growth over months. All charts are wrapped in `ResponsiveContainer` with a fixed 300px height to ensure responsiveness. The `StickyHeader` component receives the current user from `localStorage` (parsed from the "currentUser" key) and displays the page title "Service Analytics - 2025".

In the routing layer, `ServiceAnalytics` is registered at the `/service-analytics` path within the protected route structure, wrapped alongside a `Sidebar` component in `App.js`. The component also imports a `ServiceAnalytics.css` stylesheet for page-specific styling.

chooses `currentMonth` by the highest `DAU`, not by calendar order

expected_answer_keypoints: derives average session time from `totalSessionTime / sessionCount`

answer_excerpt:
The User Engagement page (`src/pages/UserEngagement.js`) is a React component that displays 2025 user engagement metrics sourced from Firebase Realtime Database. It fetches data from the `userEngagement/2025` path using Firebase's `onValue` listener for real-time updates. The page is accessible as a protected route at `/user-engagement` within the application's sidebar layout. It reads the `currentUser` from localStorage and passes it to the `StickyHeader` component for consistent navigation across pages.

The page calculates and displays six key metrics: Daily Active Users (DAU), Monthly Active Users (MAU), New Users, Returning Users, Average Session Time in minutes, and Bounce Rate percentage. Returning users are derived by subtracting new users from MAU, average session time is computed as `totalSessionTime / sessionCount`, and bounce rate is calculated as `(bounceCount / sessionCount) * 100`. The "current month" displayed in the metric cards is automatically selected as the month with the highest DAU value.

The UI consists of two main sections: a row of six metric cards with icons (using `react-icons/fa`) arranged in a 3-column responsive grid, and a monthly data table below. The table headers include tooltips (Bootstrap Tooltip components) that explain each metric when hovered, providing definitions for DAU, MAU, New Users, Returning Users, Session duration, and Bounce Rate. The current month's row is highlighted with a `table-active` CSS class for visual emphasis.

PDF links render as external anchors
non-PDF links render as preview images
tracks approval through a checkbox bound to `isApproved`

expected_answer_keypoints: renders each document link inside a large centered modal

answer_excerpt: The `VerificationModal` is a functional React component located at `src/components/VerificationModal.js` that renders a Bootstrap modal dialog for reviewing and approving/declining document submissions. It accepts props for document links (which it renders as images or PDF links depending on file extension), an approval checkbox with dynamic label, and an admin comment textarea with Approve/Decline action buttons in the footer. The component has no local state managementâ€”all state is lifted to the parent component, which passes down setters like `setComments`, `setIsApproved`, and callbacks like `onApprove` and `onDecline`. The modal uses `react-bootstrap` components (Modal, Button, Form, Row, Col) for its structure. It conditionally renders document previews: if a link ends with `.pdf`, it displays a clickable link; otherwise, it renders an `` tag with max-height styling of 250px and object-fit cover. The approval checkbox label is dynamically constructed to reference the `documentType` prop (e.g., "I have reviewed the identity and confirm it's valid"). In the parent page `HandymanVerification.js`, the modal is controlled via local state including `showVerificationModal`, `comments`, `isApproved`, `verificationType`, and `verificationLinks`. The `handleOpenVerificationModal` function populates these state values by extracting document links from the selected handyman's profile (either `photoIdCard` or `certificates`), normalizing them into an array. After the user clicks Approve or Decline, `handleDocumentApproval` writes the normalized status (lowercase) to Firebase Realtime Database under `Handyman/{handymanId}` using either `idApprovedStatus` or `certificateApprovedStatus` fields, and auto-updates the overall `verificationStatus` only if `verificationStatusManual` is not set.

PDF tables are built with `autoTable` from column headers and row accessors
column accessors can be either functions or object keys
Excel export builds a worksheet with `xlsx`
the exported blob is saved with `file-saver` as `${fileName}.xlsx`
answer_excerpt:
The `ExportReportButton` is a reusable React component located at `src/components/ExportReportButton.js` that provides dropdown-based export functionality for tabular data into two formats: PDF and Excel. It accepts three props: `data` (an array of records), `columns` (an array of column definitions with `header` and `accessor` props where accessor can be either a string or a function), and an optional `fileName` prop that defaults to "Report". The component uses `jspdf` with `jspdf-autotable` for PDF generation, `xlsx` for Excel generation, and `file-saver` for triggering downloads.

For PDF exports, the component uses landscape orientation with hardcoded column widths for indices 0 through 7, and it sets `showHead: "everyPage"` to repeat header rows across all PDF pages. For Excel exports, it builds an array-of-arrays sheet using `XLSX.utils.aoa_to_sheet` and handles missing values with the nullish coalescing operator (`?? ""`). The component depends on `react-bootstrap` for the Button and Dropdown UI elements.

This component is imported and used across multiple admin pages in the application: `UserManagement.js` uses it to export user and handyman data, `SupportFeedback.js` uses it for support ticket reports, and `UserVerification.js` uses it for exporting user verification data. In all these pages, it's imported with a relative path: `import ExportReportButton from "../components/ExportReportButton"`.

keypoint_hits.keypoint: returns `null` when there is one page or less
previous and next buttons disable at the page bounds
it renders first/last/nearby pages with ellipses in the middle

expected_answer_keypoints: returns `null` when there is one page or less
previous and next buttons disable at the page bounds
it renders first/last/nearby pages with ellipses in the middle
answer_excerpt:
The PaginationControls component in `src/components/PaginationControls.js` renders page buttons using a map over a calculated number of pages. The total page count is derived from `Math.ceil(totalItems / entriesPerPage)`, and the component bails out entirely by returning `null` when `totalPages <= 1`. The core decision logic for each page number is: show it if it is within Â±2 of the current page, OR if it is the first or last page (page 1 or `totalPages`).

The ellipsis display logic is narrow in scopeâ€”it only places a `...` span immediately before page 2 or immediately after page `totalPages - 1` when those boundary pages themselves fall outside the Â±2 visibility window. Pages more than 2 away from the current page, and not being page 1 or `totalPages`, are skipped entirely via `return null`. This means middle pages in large page counts may not show ellipsis between the visible window and the edge pages.

The current page button is styled distinctly with a `primary` variant, white text, rounded corners, and bold weight, while all other page buttons use the `link` variant with dark text. Page numbers are zero-padded to two digits using `String(page).padStart(2, "0")`, so users see "01", "02", etc.

One limitation: this implementation does not show ellipsis between the Â±2 window and the boundary pages when there is a gap beyond Â±2 from current. For example, if totalPages = 20 and currentPage = 10, pages 8â€“12 render, then page 19 shows as a button with no intermediate ellipsis before it.

The `FirebaseTest` page component is a React component located at `src/pages/FirebaseTest.js` that serves as a test panel for validating Firebase Realtime Database operations and real-time synchronization. It exports a default `FirebaseTest` component that provides a UI with write/read buttons and live data tables styled with Bootstrap. The component depends on React hooks (`useState`), Firebase Realtime Database functions (`ref`, `set`, `push`, `onValue`), the database instance from `../firebase`, and mock admin data from `../data/mockUsers`.

The component implements three `useEffect` hooks with `onValue` listeners that maintain real-time synchronization for jobs and admin users. Write operations are handled by three distinct functions: `handleWriteTestData` for general test data, `handleAddJob` which uses the `push` method for creating new job entries, and `handleUploadAdminUsers` which iteratively uses `set` to write multiple admin user records. When displaying admin users in the UI, the component filters out any records under the "test" node.

The route to this component is defined in `src/App.js` as `/firebase-test`, making it accessible at that URL path. This route is defined outside the protected layout wrapper, meaning it does not render with the Sidebar component unlike other admin pages. The component is intended purely for development and testing purposes to verify that Firebase database connections and CRUD operations are functioning correctly before relying on them in production features.

`Sidebar.js` links match the nested routes for dashboard, verification, management, analytics, admin settings, and support
`/firebase-test` exists as a separate route outside the protected layout

expected_answer_keypoints: `App.js` nests dashboard pages inside a protected `path="/*"` layout that renders `Sidebar`
`Sidebar.js` links match the nested routes for dashboard, verification, management, analytics, admin settings, and support
`/firebase-test` exists as a separate route outside the protected layout
answer_excerpt:
The dashboard routes are organized in a nested routing pattern within the main `App.js` file. All protected routes, including the dashboard, are wrapped in a catch-all wildcard route (`/*`) that places them inside a flex container with a `Sidebar` component on the left and a content area on the right. The root route (`/`) renders the `Login` page, while the actual dashboard is located at `/dashboard` and renders the `Dashboard` component. Protected routes are defined inside a nested `<Routes>` block within the catch-all route, allowing them to share the sidebar layout.

The routing structure uses React Router v6 and includes several related dashboard routes alongside the main dashboard: `/handyman-verification`, `/user-verification`, `/user-management`, `/job-management`, `/service-analytics`, `/user-engagement`, `/admin-settings`, and `/support-feedback`. All of these routes render their respective page components (e.g., `HandymanVerification`, `UserManagement`, `JobManagement`). The `Sidebar` component uses React Router's `NavLink` to provide active route styling and links to each of these dashboard sub-routes.

The entry point in `src/index.js` wraps the entire application with `BrowserRouter` and `UserProvider` before rendering the `App` component. The `Dashboard` component itself fetches analytics data from Firebase in real-time using `onValue` and renders stats, bar charts, and pie charts displaying job trends, user registrations, top locations, and performer rankings. Bootstrap and Bootstrap Icons are imported globally in `index.js`, making them available throughout the entire application including the dashboard.

The routing also includes a test route at `/firebase-test` for testing Firebase functionality separately from the protected routes. The `ForgotPassword` route at `/forgot-password` is outside the protected layout, similar to the login route.

keypoint_hits.keypoint: `App` still mounts the protected layout shell around those pages

expected_answer_keypoints: `App` still mounts the protected layout shell around those pages
answer_excerpt:
The sidebar visibility is controlled through a two-layer auth check: the component-level guard in `Sidebar.js` and the routing structure in `App.js`. The `Sidebar` component imports `useUser` from `UserContext` and immediately checks `if (!currentUser)` â€” when there's no authenticated user it returns `null`, effectively hiding the sidebar from the DOM . The `currentUser` value comes from `UserContext`, which loads it from localStorage under the `"currentUser"` key when the `UserProvider` mounts . This means the sidebar won't render on any page until a user object exists in localStorage.

The routing architecture in `App.js` reinforces this by placing the sidebar only within the protected route block. Login (`/`), forgot-password (`/forgot-password`), and the Firebase test route are all rendered outside the sidebar layout, while every other route (`/*`) is wrapped in a flex container containing `<Sidebar />` alongside the page content . When a user successfully authenticates through `Login.js`, the component stores `currentUser`, `isLoggedIn`, and `userRole` in localStorage, then navigates to `/dashboard` . Because `UserProvider` wraps the entire app, the `currentUser` value becomes available immediately after the localStorage read in `useEffect`, and the sidebar renders on all subsequent protected routes.

The end result is a straightforward pattern: unauthenticated users see only the login page with no sidebar, and authenticated users get the sidebar on every protected page. There is no explicit logout mechanism shown in the snippets, but clearing `currentUser` from localStorage would trigger the sidebar guard to hide it on the next load.

keypoint_hits.keypoint: it is also reused by `UserManagement` and `UserVerification`

expected_answer_keypoints: it is also reused by `UserManagement` and `UserVerification`

answer_excerpt:
The `ExportReportButton` is a reusable React component located at `src/components/ExportReportButton.js` that provides dropdown export functionality for tabular data. It supports exporting to two formats: PDF with landscape orientation using `jspdf` and `jspdf-autotable`, and Excel using the `xlsx` library and `file-saver`. The component accepts three props: `data` (an array of data objects), `columns` (an array of column definitions with `header` and `accessor` props where accessor can be either a string or function), and an optional `fileName` prop defaulting to "Report".

The PDF export implementation uses landscape orientation with hardcoded column widths for indices 0â€“7, and includes `showHead: "everyPage"` to repeat header rows across PDF pages. For Excel exports, the component builds an array-of-arrays sheet via `XLSX.utils.aoa_to_sheet`. Both export methods handle missing values using the nullish coalescing operator (`?? ""`) to provide empty string fallbacks.

This component is imported and used across multiple pages in the application: `SupportFeedback.js` for exporting support ticket reports, `UserManagement.js` for user/handyman data exports, and `UserVerification.js` for exporting verification status reports. All these pages follow a consistent pattern where `ExportReportButton` receives the filtered data and corresponding column definitions from their respective state management.

answer_excerpt:
The `ConfirmModal` component is a reusable confirmation dialog located at `src/components/ConfirmModal.js`. It is exported as a default export, so the correct import statement is `import ConfirmModal from "../components/ConfirmModal";` when importing from a page component in the `src/pages/` directory. ConfirmModal is exported as a default export from `src/components/ConfirmModal.js`. The standard import path from page components is `"../components/ConfirmModal"`.

The component accepts the following props: `show` (boolean to control visibility), `onHide` (callback when closing), `onConfirm` (callback when confirming), `title` (defaults to "Confirm Action"), `body` (defaults to "Are you sure you want to proceed?"), `loading` (boolean to show spinner and disable buttons), `confirmText` (defaults to "Confirm"), and `cancelText` (defaults to "Cancel"). ConfirmModal uses default values for title, body, confirmText, and cancelText props. The loading prop displays a spinner and "Saving..." text while disabling both buttons.

This component is used consistently across the codebase's verification pages as a safety pattern before write operations. Both `HandymanVerification.js` and `UserVerification.js` import it from the same path and use the `openConfirm` helper pattern to wrap destructive or status-changing actions with a confirmation dialog. HandymanVerification.js imports ConfirmModal from "../components/ConfirmModal". UserVerification.js imports ConfirmModal from "../components/ConfirmModal". AdminSettings.js imports ConfirmModal from "../components/ConfirmModal". The pages use the confirm modal as a safety guard before database write operations.

keypoint_hits.keypoint: `ServiceAnalytics.js` uses `database` for analytics, users, and jobs

expected_answer_keypoints: `ServiceAnalytics.js` uses `database` for analytics, users, and jobs
answer_excerpt:
The Firebase login functionality in this codebase uses Firebase v9+ modular imports. The core Firebase configuration is centralized in `src/firebase.js`, which exports three services: `database` (Realtime Database), `storage` (Cloud Storage), and `db` (Firestore). The `database` export is the instance used for all login-related authentication queries. In the login page (`src/pages/Login.js`), Firebase is imported by pulling the `database` export from the local firebase module, then using `ref`, `get`, and `child` functions from `firebase/database` to query the admin node. The authentication flow queries the `admin` node in Realtime Database, filters out any entry with key `"test"`, and then matches the entered email and password against the stored credentials. On successful authentication, the component checks that `foundUser.status === "active"` before storing `currentUser`, `isLoggedIn`, and `userRole` in localStorage and navigating to the dashboard. Email validation uses a regex pattern `/^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/` and password requires a minimum length of 6 characters. The login component uses `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` to render error messages containing `
` tags, which is a potential XSS vector if error content ever becomes user-controlled. The Realtime Database path structure stores admin users under `/admin` with each admin having properties including `email`, `password`, `status`, and `role`.

`ServiceAnalytics` passes the normalized `chartData` array into it
`ServiceAnalytics` renders six line charts through this component
the six data keys are `newHandymen`, `newCustomers`, `newUsers`, `jobsCompleted`, `averageRating`, and `totalLogins`
answer_excerpt:
ServiceAnalytics is a React page component located at `src/pages/ServiceAnalytics.js` that serves as the main analytics dashboard for visualizing 2025 service metrics. It fetches data from four Firebase database paths: `serviceAnalytics/2025` for monthly analytics, `Handyman` for handyman counts, `User` for customer counts, and `Job` for job status distribution. The component uses `useState` to manage four data states (`chartData`, `userStats`, `jobStats`, `ratingDistribution`) and `useEffect` with an async `fetchData` function to load data on mount. It formats monthly data using a `monthsOrder` array mapping the twelve months, extracting fields like `newHandymen`, `newCustomers`, `newUsers`, `jobsCompleted`, `averageRating`, and `totalLogins`.

The dashboard renders four distinct visualizations using the recharts library. A PieChart displays the user ratio between Handymen and Customers, calculated by counting the keys in each Firebase snapshot. A BarChart visualizes job status distribution by iterating through Job records and incrementing counters for "Done", "In Progress", and "Cancelled" statuses. A second PieChart shows rating distribution, though this data is currently mock data as noted in a comment indicating no individual ratings exist yet. A fourth chart (truncated in the snippet) renders user growth trends from the formatted `chartData`.

The component integrates with the application's routing via `src/App.js`, where it is imported and assigned to the protected route `/service-analytics`. It is nested within the sidebar layout alongside other admin pages like Dashboard, HandymanVerification, and JobManagement. The component receives the `currentUser` from localStorage and passes it to the StickyHeader component for consistent navigation across the dashboard.

keypoint_hits.keypoint: `AdminProfileHeader` shows full name, email, and role badge

expected_answer_keypoints: `AdminProfileHeader` shows full name, email, and role badge
answer_excerpt:
The admin profile avatar and footer functionality are implemented through the `AdminProfileHeader` component, which is imported and rendered within the `Sidebar` component. The `AdminProfileHeader` displays a circular avatar image, the user's full name, email, and a role badge. In `AdminProfileHeader`, the container uses the class `mt-auto pt-3`, which applies flexbox margin-top auto to push the profile section to the bottom of the sidebar, functioning as a footer. The avatar uses a hardcoded Firebase storage URL for a default image, and the component expects a `user` prop containing `firstName`, `lastName`, `email`, and `role` properties.

The `Sidebar` component conditionally renders based on authentication status. It imports `AdminProfileHeader` from the sibling components directory and uses the `useUser` hook from `UserContext` to access the `currentUser` data. If no authenticated user exists, the `Sidebar` returns `null`, guarding against unauthenticated access. The sidebar navigation uses `NavLink` from React Router for active route styling and groups links into three sections (core features, analytics, settings) separated by `<hr>` elements.

The `App.js` routing structure wraps all protected routes in a flex container containing the `Sidebar`, ensuring the admin profile footer appears consistently across authenticated pages. The wildcard route (`/*`) nests all protected pages inside a `d-flex` wrapper where the `Sidebar` serves as the left navigation column. There's also a separate `StickyHeader` component used on pages that provides an additional profile avatar display with notification bell, though this appears to be a page-level header rather than part of the sidebar footer layout.

`Dashboard` renders category overview from `dashboard_content.job_overview` instead of reusing `JobCategoryStats`

`Dashboard` renders category overview from `dashboard_content.job_overview` instead of reusing `JobCategoryStats`
`Dashboard` imports `JobCategoryStats` but does not render it in JSX
answer_excerpt:
The job category definitions are centralized in a single constants file that serves as the shared source of truth across the application. The file `src/constants/jobCategories.js` exports a static array called `JOB_CATEGORIES` containing 16 predefined service category strings such as "AC Repair Services", "Appliance Repair", "Cleaning Solution", "Beauty & Wellness", and "Electric & Plumbing". This array has no external dependencies, making it a standalone configuration module that can be imported anywhere categories are needed.

Components consume this shared definition in different ways depending on their purpose. The `JobManagement.js` page imports `JOB_CATEGORIES` directly and uses it to populate filter dropdowns and validate category selections when users filter job listings. It matches the imported constant against the `jobCat` field stored in Firebase job records. Meanwhile, `JobCategoryStats.js` works differentlyâ€”it dynamically reads the `jobCat` values from live Firebase data and builds a category map by iterating over job records, treating any string found as a valid category. This component aggregates active and quoted counts per category without depending on the constants file.

There appears to be a data consistency gap worth noting. The mock data in `jobData.js` uses categories like "Plumbing", "Cleaning", "Carpentry", and "Electrical", but these don't fully align with the 16 categories defined in `jobCategories.js`. The constant file defines categories like "Cleaning Solution" and "Electric & Plumbing" while the mock data uses different naming conventions. This mismatch means a filter using the `JOB_CATEGORIES` constant might not correctly match mock jobs, though the dynamic stats component would still aggregate them since it reads whatever values exist in the database. The `JobManagement` component itself includes a TODO comment indicating pending alignment work with a teammate regarding data field formatting across the app.

keypoint_hits.keypoint: `UserProvider` rehydrates `currentUser` from localStorage and exposes it through context

multiple pages still read `currentUser` directly from localStorage instead of the context

expected_answer_keypoints: `UserProvider` rehydrates `currentUser` from localStorage and exposes it through context
`Sidebar` is the shell component that uses `useUser()` to decide visibility
multiple pages still read `currentUser` directly from localStorage instead of the context
`App` has no route-level auth guard beyond rendering the shared layout shell
answer_excerpt:
The application centralizes user session state through a React Context pattern defined in `src/context/UserContext.js`. The `UserProvider` component wraps the entire application at the root level (in `src/index.js`), making user state available globally via the `useUser` hook. This context maintains `currentUser` and `setCurrentUser` in component state, providing a consistent interface for all child components to access and update the authenticated user.

Session persistence is handled through localStorage under the `"currentUser"` key. The `UserProvider` loads the stored user on mount by retrieving and parsing the JSON from localStorage. During login (in `src/pages/Login.js`), successful authentication writes the user object, `isLoggedIn` flag, and `userRole` to localStorage, which the context then picks up on subsequent page loads. Components like `Sidebar.js` consume this context to conditionally render based on authentication status.

The architecture follows a straightforward provider pattern where the context serves as the single source of truth for user session data throughout the application, with localStorage acting as the persistence layer.

The codebase implements role-based access control for admin settings, but the evidence provides only a partial view of the implementation. The `AdminSettings` component explicitly restricts management capabilities to users with the "Master Admin" role, using Firebase real-time listeners on the `admin` path to sync admin data. The component includes filtering by role and status, confirming this is a centralized admin management interface.

The permission system is defined through user objects with both a singular `role` field and a `roles` array for granular permissions. For example, the mock data shows three permission tiers: `master1` has `["manage_users", "edit_admins", "all_access"]`, `admin1` has `["manage_users", "view_reports", "verify_handymen", "reply_support"]`, and `staff1` has only `["verify_handymen", "reply_support"]`. This structure suggests a capability-based model where different roles accumulate specific permissions.

However, the actual permission-checking logic (where the code evaluates `user.roles` or `user.role` before granting access) is not visible in the provided snippets. The `Sidebar` component shows that the Admin Settings navigation link exists and is likely protected by the route wrapper, but the enforcement mechanism isn't demonstrated. To verify your access control implementation, you would need to examine the `UserContext` to see how user state is validated, and any higher-order components or custom hooks that guard protected routes or features based on role/permission checks.

keypoint_hits.keypoint: `VerificationModal` drives approve/decline actions for identity or certificate documents
automatic `verificationStatus` recalculation only runs when `verificationStatusManual` is not set
manual dropdown confirmation writes `verificationStatus` and `verificationStatusManual: true`
the manual approval path warns if all documents are not already approved

expected_answer_keypoints: `VerificationModal` drives approve/decline actions for identity or certificate documents
automatic `verificationStatus` recalculation only runs when `verificationStatusManual` is not set
manual dropdown confirmation writes `verificationStatus` and `verificationStatusManual: true`
the manual approval path warns if all documents are not already approved
answer_excerpt:
The handyman document verification flow is implemented primarily in `HandymanVerification.js` through the `handleDocumentApproval` async function. When an admin reviews a document and triggers approval or decline, this function normalizes the status to lowercase, then writes either `idApprovedStatus` (for identity cards) or `certificateApprovedStatus` (for certificates) to the Firebase Realtime Database under the `Handyman/{handymanId}` path. The function uses the `update` method from Firebase to write to the database.

After updating the individual document status, the function checks whether the `verificationStatusManual` override flag is set on the handyman record. If manual override is false or not set, the function automatically computes a new overall `verificationStatus` based on both document statuses: it becomes "approved" only when both the identity and certificate statuses are "approved," and becomes "declined" if either document is "declined." The `verificationStatusManual` flag exists as an override mechanism to prevent automatic status updates, allowing admins to manually control the final status independently of document approvals.

The `VerificationModal` component (default export from `src/components/VerificationModal.js`) provides the UI layer for document review. It renders document previewsâ€”displaying images inline or providing PDF linksâ€”alongside an approval checkbox labeled with the document type being reviewed. The modal accepts props for `onApprove` and `onDecline` callbacks, along with optional admin comments, and passes state back to the parent component via lifted state handlers. The modal's footer presents Decline (danger variant) and Approve (success variant) buttons, which trigger the respective callbacks that eventually call `handleDocumentApproval` in the parent component.

The notification system within `handleDocumentApproval` provides user feedback: on success it shows a timed notification (2.5 seconds) indicating which document type was updated and to what status, using a...
